long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.name: Dynamic Start
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria: The first shot in the video changes in less than 3 seconds.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.rationale: The first shot changes before 3 seconds. The opening frame shows Mae the cat on a bookshelf. By the next sampled frame, the view has changed to a text-overlaid shot reading "Never run out of food," indicating a new shot. Based on the sampled evidence, the first shot change occurs at approximately 0:02, which is under the 3-second threshold.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.evidence: Opening shot: cat on bookshelf labeled "Mae". First visible change: around 0:02 to a text card/closer cropped scene with "Never run out of food."
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.strengths: Clear visual contrast between the initial cat shot and the following text-overlaid shot; change occurs early in the video.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.weaknesses: Exact cut time is approximate because only sampled frames are provided, not a full frame-by-frame timeline.

long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.name: Quick Pacing
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Within ANY 5 consecutive seconds there are 5 or more shots in the video. These include hard cuts, soft
                transitions and camera changes such as camera pans, swipes, zooms, depth of field changes, tracking shots
                and movement of the camera.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.rationale: From the provided sampled frames and transcript, there is evidence of at least one early cut, but not enough evidence to verify 5 or more distinct shots within any 5-second window. The visible sequence suggests a small number of shots rather than very rapid pacing. Therefore, this criterion is not verifiably met.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.evidence: Observed frames suggest an initial cat shot followed by a text shot, but no evidence of 5 distinct shot changes within any 5-second interval was provided.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.strengths: The sampled frames allow identification of at least some shot change.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.weaknesses: Insufficient frame sampling to confidently count 5+ shot changes in a 5-second span; several transitions could have been missed.

long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.name: Quick Pacing (First 5 seconds)
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
There are at least 5 shot changes or visual cuts detected in the first 5 seconds of the video. These include hard cuts,
                soft transitions and camera changes such as camera pans, swipes, zooms, depth of field changes,
                tracking shots and movement of the camera.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.rationale: The provided evidence shows an early cut from the cat shot to a text-heavy shot, but it does not show 5 or more shot changes within the first 5 seconds. Since the available sampled frames are limited and do not document five distinct transitions in that interval, the requirement is not verified.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.evidence: First sampled frame: cat on shelf with "Mae" label. Second sampled frame: "Never run out of food" text card/scene. No additional distinct shot changes are visible in the first 5 seconds from the supplied frames.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.strengths: At least one clear early visual change is observable.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.weaknesses: The frame samples are too sparse to establish 5 separate cuts/shot changes in the first 5 seconds.

long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.name: Supers
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria: Any supers (text overlays) have been incorporated at any time in the video.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.rationale: Text overlays are clearly visible in the sampled frames. The first frame shows the supers 'Mae' with an arrow pointing to the cat. Another frame shows 'Never run out of food.' These are incorporated during the video, so the feature is present.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.evidence: ~0:00-0:02: 'Mae'; ~0:03-0:06: 'Never run out of food'.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.strengths: Text is large, high-contrast, and unambiguous.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.weaknesses: Exact frame-by-frame boundaries are approximate because only sampled frames are provided.

long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.name: Supers with Audio
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
The speech heard in the audio of the video matches OR is contextually supportive of the overlaid
                text shown on screen.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.rationale: The transcript directly supports the overlays. The on-screen 'Mae' matches the spoken 'Meet Mae,' and 'Never run out of food' matches 'With Chewy, she never runs out of the food she loves.' The speech is clearly contextually aligned with the supers.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.evidence: Supers at ~0:00-0:02 ('Mae') supported by speech at the start ('Meet Mae'); supers at ~0:03-0:06 ('Never run out of food') supported by speech 'she never runs out of the food she loves'.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.strengths: Strong direct correspondence between transcript and overlays.
long_form_abcd_evaluated_features.weaknesses: Timing alignment is inferred from transcript rather than exact audio timestamps.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Tight Framing & Visual Dominance
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Quantifies the spatial dominance of the primary subject.
                Tight framing is defined by a Subject-to-Frame Ratio (SfR) of ≥60%.
                The score reflects the 'Density' (persistence) of tight framing, 
                differentiating between incidental close-ups and thematic visual dominance.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: The video repeatedly uses close framing on the cat 'Mae' and on tight text/leg crop shots. The opening shot frames Mae large in the frame, with the cat occupying most of the right side. A later shot shows only the lower body/feet and an overlaid message, which is also visually tight. The transcript reinforces the subject focus on Mae, but the visual evidence is strongest for animal close-ups rather than full-body or wide context shots.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: Frame 1 shows Mae filling a large portion of the frame while perched on the cat tree; the face and torso dominate the composition. Frame 2 is a tight crop with large text and the subject partially occluded behind it. Frame 3 is a low, close crop of the human's legs and shoes, but the main thematic subject remains the tightly framed pet-story visuals.
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Clear close-up dominance in the opening and mid-video visuals; strong mobile-friendly framing with the subject occupying substantial frame area.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: Available sampled frames are limited, so exact duration-based density cannot be fully verified from the evidence provided; some shots are text-led rather than subject-led.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Human Voice Presence
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Quantifies the presence, duration, and quality of human speech. 
            Voice includes Voice-Overs (VO), direct-to-camera dialogue, or background 
            narration. The metric measures 'Vocal Density' (percentage of video containing 
            speech) and assesses the clarity and role of the speaker.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: The transcript contains continuous spoken narration for nearly the entire ad: 'Meet Mae...' through 'there's Chewy.' This is clear human voiceover, likely spanning almost the full 15 seconds. The speech is straightforward and fully intelligible from the transcript, with no evidence of heavy distortion.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: Transcript: 'Meet Mae, who never runs out of things to climb. With Chewy, she never runs out of the food she loves, and her human never has to run out to the store. Climb away, Mae. For low prices, for life with pets, there's Chewy.'
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: High speech density; clearly narrated brand message; transcript indicates a complete voiced spot.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: Background music/noise cannot be directly assessed from the provided materials, so vocal clarity is inferred from transcript readability rather than audio signal quality.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Direct to Camera
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Quantifies the duration and intensity of direct eye contact between the 
                on-screen subject and the camera lens. This feature measures the 
                'Direct Address Density' and assesses the intimacy of the framing 
                (e.g., face-to-face address).
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: No clear evidence shows a human subject looking directly into the camera lens. The visible human is only shown from the legs/feet, not the face, and the cat is not a reliable direct-address subject for this feature. The transcript is voiced narration, but direct eye contact with the viewer is not verifiable from the sampled frames.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: Frame 3 shows a person's lower body only; no face or eyes are visible. Frame 1 shows the cat facing downward/outward, but there is no definitive lens engagement. The transcript does not indicate direct-to-camera delivery.
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Narration is present, which supports an addressed ad style.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: No visible facial eye contact; no on-camera speaker identified; direct address cannot be verified.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Supers & Text-Audio Synchronicity
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Quantifies the presence, accuracy, and synchronization of text overlays (supers) 
        with the spoken audio. This measures 'Text Density' and the 'Synchronicity Score' 
        to determine how effectively the visual text reinforces the spoken message.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: Text overlays are clearly visible in the sampled frames, including 'Mae' and 'Never run out of food.' These supers directly reinforce the transcript and appear synchronized with the spoken narration about Mae and Chewy's food supply message.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: Frame 1 includes the label 'Mae' with an arrow pointing to the cat. Frame 2 displays 'Never run out of food,' matching the transcript's line 'With Chewy, she never runs out of the food she loves.'
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Clear readable text; strong semantic match to the spoken copy; text is central and easy to see on mobile.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: Only sampled frames are provided, so exact on-screen duration cannot be measured precisely; likely static callouts rather than dynamic captions.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Product Close-Up
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Quantifies segments where the product occupies at least 30% of the frame. 
            This measures standard product visibility and presence within a recognizable 
            context or environment.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: No actual Chewy product package or branded pet food item is visible in the sampled frames. The video appears to communicate the brand promise verbally and through text rather than showing a physical product close-up occupying 30% to 59% of the frame.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: The sampled frames show a cat on a shelf, text overlays, and a person's legs, but no visible Chewy product container or package. The transcript mentions food and Chewy generally, but not a visible product shot.
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Brand message is clear in the transcript.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: No identifiable product close-up; product presence is only implied, not visually shown.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Product Extreme Close-Up
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Quantifies segments where the product is the dominant visual element, 
            occupying 60% or more of the frame. This measures 'Macro' focus and 
            high-detail product showcasing.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: There is no evidence of any branded product occupying 60% or more of the frame. The visuals center on the cat, interior setting, and text overlays rather than a product macro shot.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: All provided frames lack a visible Chewy product hero shot; no package, label, or macro product detail is shown.
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Clear absence of a product macro focus is easy to verify from the sampled frames.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: None significant; evidence is sufficient to rule this out.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Product Context & Usage Quality
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Evaluates the 'Show, Don't Tell' quality. Quantifies physical 
            interaction, environmental realism, and utility demonstration. 
            Measures both the duration of usage (Density) and the effectiveness 
            of the demonstration (Quality Score).
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: The ad discusses Chewy's convenience and food supply, but the provided evidence does not show the product being handled, consumed, or operated. There is no visible product-use demonstration, so 'product-in-use' is not verifiable from the sampled frames and transcript alone.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: Transcript mentions 'With Chewy, she never runs out of the food she loves,' but no on-screen feeding, ordering, or package interaction is shown in the sampled frames.
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Real-home environment and pet-centered context are present.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: No direct physical interaction with a product; utility is told rather than shown.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Casual Language
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Quantifies the informality of the script. Measures the use of everyday language, 
            slang, contractions, and conversational filler vs. formal/corporate scripted speech.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: The transcript uses conversational, everyday phrasing such as 'Meet Mae,' 'never runs out,' 'run out to the store,' and 'Climb away, Mae.' The tone is friendly and simple rather than corporate or technical, though it remains scripted ad copy rather than spontaneous speech.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: Transcript: 'Meet Mae, who never runs out of things to climb...' and 'With Chewy, she never runs out of the food she loves...'
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Clear plain-language wording; friendly and accessible tone.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: Not much slang or filler, so it reads as polished conversational advertising rather than truly spontaneous speech.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Humor & Comedic Timing
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Detects and quantifies attempts at humor, including wit, physical comedy, 
        satire, or comedic timing.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: The video does not present a clear joke, gag, or comedic payoff in the sampled evidence. The line 'never runs out of things to climb' is playful, but it functions more as a pet-character descriptor than an explicit humor device.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: Transcript is brand-benefit focused and earnest; sampled frames show a cat climbing and text overlays, with no obvious punchline or physical comedy.
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: A light, playful tone exists in the copy.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: No strong comedic mechanism or timing is visible.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Character-Driven
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Video features a relatable character whose journey or transformation resonates with audience.
            Evaluates character prominence, relatability, and narrative journey shown.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: Mae is clearly the central character named in the opening line and visually featured in the frames. The narrative is built around her behavior and needs, creating a simple character-led story arc about climbing and food convenience. The cat is relatable as a pet persona, even though the 'journey' is minimal.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: Transcript opens with 'Meet Mae' and repeatedly refers to her. Frame 1 labels the cat 'Mae' with an arrow, making her the explicit protagonist.
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Strong character prominence; clear naming and repeated reference; pet-centered relatability.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: Transformation/journey is limited; the story is more of a branded vignette than a developed arc.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Call to Action (Audio)
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Detects and quantifies spoken instructions that direct the viewer to take 
        action. This includes verbal commands from Voice-Overs (VO) or on-screen talent. 
        Measures 'CTA Density' and 'Urgency Level' to determine the strength of the 
        conversion signal.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: The transcript ends with a brand statement rather than a direct command. 'For low prices, for life with pets, there's Chewy' is promotional, but it is not an imperative CTA such as shop, visit, or buy.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: Final transcript line: 'For low prices, for life with pets, there's Chewy.' No direct viewer instruction is present.
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Clear brand mention and value proposition.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: No explicit action verb or ask.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Special Offer (Speech)
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Audio/voiceover explicitly announces special offer, discount, or deal.
            Evaluates clarity of offer type, specific details mentioned, and delivery emphasis.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: The transcript explicitly mentions a pricing offer: 'For low prices, for life with pets, there's Chewy.' This is an offer/value claim, but it does not specify a numeric discount or limited-time promotion. It is delivered as a closing brand value statement.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: Transcript includes 'For low prices, for life with pets, there's Chewy.'
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Clear price-related promotional language; voiced in the closing line.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: No specific discount percentage, promo code, or time-bound deal is stated.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Production Style
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Quantifies the visual 'Lo-Fi' vs. 'Hi-Fi' characteristics of the video. 
        Measures the presence of UGC (User Generated Content) markers such as 
        handheld camera movement, natural lighting, and native mobile aesthetics. 
        Assesses if the video feels like an 'organic post' or a 'produced commercial.'
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: The sampled frames show a polished, composited commercial style with clean framing, designed text overlays, and controlled lighting rather than handheld mobile footage. This looks more like a produced ad than native UGC.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: The visuals use crisp typography, stable composition, and curated interior set design. Frame 2 and Frame 3 especially resemble designed ad graphics rather than casual phone capture.
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Clear commercial polish is visible.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: Limited frame sampling prevents a full motion-style assessment, though the overall look is still evidently produced.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Short Form Video Adaptation
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Quantifies the 'Native Emulation' of the production. Measures how effectively 
            the video mimics organic social content through lo-fi aesthetics, handheld 
            camera physics, and non-commercial editing patterns.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: The video does not strongly mimic organic social content. The presence of polished supers, controlled composition, and ad-like pacing suggest a commercial-hybrid rather than a native post style.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: Text overlays are clean and centrally placed; the shots appear staged and curated rather than handheld or lo-fi.
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Some short-form brevity and vertical framing support platform fit.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: Overall execution remains commercial and polished, not truly native/organic.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Emoji Usage
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Detects intentional creative emoji use: 1. Standard characters in text, 
            2. Animated effects, 3. Emoji-style stickers/graphics, 
            4. Platform-specific features. Excludes incidental background captures.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: No emojis are visible in the sampled frames, and none are indicated in the transcript.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: Visible overlays include 'Mae' and 'Never run out of food' only, with no emoji characters or stickers.
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Absence of emoji use is clear from the evidence provided.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: None.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Direct to Camera Character Talk
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Evaluates the intimacy and continuity of direct lens address. 
            Measures the 'Breaking of the Fourth Wall' through gaze and conversational delivery.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: There is no clear evidence of a character speaking directly to camera. The ad uses voiceover and pet imagery rather than a visible person addressing the viewer with eye contact.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: No face-forward speaking subject is shown in the sampled frames; the transcript is third-person narration about Mae, not direct viewer address by a character.
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Conversational narration creates an intimate tone.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: No visible direct-lens character talk is verifiable.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Brand Secondary Element
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Evaluates if the brand is positioned as a secondary, natural element. 
            High scores indicate the brand feels like part of the environment, not a forced ad.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: Chewy is integrated as a narrative solution rather than a forced foreground product shot. The story is centered on Mae, with Chewy mentioned as the supporting brand that solves the food-running-out problem.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: Transcript frames Chewy as the helper: 'With Chewy, she never runs out of the food she loves...' and the visuals keep Mae as the main focus rather than the logo or packaging.
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Brand is secondary to the pet character and story.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: Still clearly an ad, so the brand integration is not fully invisible or organic.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Everyday Persona Validation
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Determines if the video is led by a relatable 'everyday person' or creator. 
            Returns negative if the character is a professional actor, celebrity, 
            or fictional/animated entity.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: The primary character is a cat, not an everyday person or creator. No human everyday-person protagonist is identifiable in the sampled frames or transcript.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: The video centers on Mae the cat, named explicitly in the overlay and transcript. The human appears only as legs/feet, not as a person-driven creator narrative.
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Clear identification of the protagonist as an animal mascot/subject rather than a human creator.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: Some human presence exists, but it is not sufficient to classify the lead as an everyday person.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Secondary Product Context
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria:
Evaluates if the product is positioned as a secondary element rather than the main focus of the ad, 
            appearing in a natural and realistic context.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: Although the setting feels like a lived-in home, there is no visible product being used naturally in the sampled frames. The product/function is described in voiceover but not shown as an active secondary prop or background action.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: Home interior with cat tree and furniture is visible, but no pet food bag, bowl, or ordering interaction appears in the provided frames.
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Realistic home environment supports natural product context in principle.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: No direct product usage is visible, so the feature cannot be confirmed as present.

shorts_evaluated_features.name: Vertical Format Designed For Mobile
shorts_evaluated_features.evaluation_criteria: Verifies 9:16 portrait ratio and detects letterboxing/pillarboxing.
shorts_evaluated_features.rationale: The provided frames are clearly in a vertical portrait layout optimized for mobile viewing, with no visible letterboxing. The composition fits a 9:16 short-form format.
shorts_evaluated_features.evidence: All sampled frames are tall vertical images consistent with mobile shorts formatting.
shorts_evaluated_features.strengths: Strong evidence of portrait orientation and mobile-safe composition.
shorts_evaluated_features.weaknesses: Safe-zone compliance is inferred from the layout, but exact UI overlap cannot be fully tested without platform context.
